stem: What is the largest eigenvalue of \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ 0 & [[d]] \end{pmatrix} \)?
solution: For a triangular matrix, the eigenvalues are exactly its diagonal entries \( [[a]] \) and \( [[d]] \); the larger of the two is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is one of the two eigenvalues, but not necessarily the larger one.
This is the other eigenvalue, but not necessarily the larger one.
This is the trace (sum of both eigenvalues), not a single eigenvalue.
This is the off-diagonal entry, which is not an eigenvalue of a triangular matrix.
This is one more than the correct largest eigenvalue.
This is one less than the correct largest eigenvalue.

stem: What is the sum of the eigenvalues of \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ 0 & [[d]] & [[e]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[f]] \end{pmatrix} \)?
solution: The eigenvalues of this triangular matrix are its diagonal entries \( [[a]], [[d]], [[f]] \), so their sum is \( [[a]]+[[d]]+[[f]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the PRODUCT of the eigenvalues, not their sum.
This sums the off-diagonal entries instead of the diagonal (eigenvalue) entries.
This omits the third eigenvalue \( [[f]] \) from the sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by two from the correct sum.

stem: What is the product of the eigenvalues of \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ 0 & [[d]] & [[e]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[f]] \end{pmatrix} \)?
solution: The eigenvalues are the diagonal entries \( [[a]], [[d]], [[f]] \), so their product is \( ([[a]])([[d]])([[f]]) = [[answer]] \) (this equals \( \det(A) \)).
distractor_rationales: This is the SUM of the eigenvalues (the trace), not their product.
This has the right magnitude but the wrong sign.
This multiplies entries from the first row instead of the diagonal.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[s]] & [[prod]] \\ -1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \) (none of its entries is obviously an eigenvalue). What is the largest eigenvalue of \( A \)?
solution: The characteristic polynomial is \( \lambda^2 - ([[s]])\lambda + ([[prod]]) = 0 \), which factors as \( (\lambda-([[p]]))(\lambda-([[q]])) = 0 \), giving eigenvalues \( [[p]] \) and \( [[q]] \). The larger one is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the trace of \( A \) (the sum of both eigenvalues), not a single eigenvalue.
This is the determinant of \( A \) (the product of both eigenvalues), not a single eigenvalue.
This halves the trace, which only gives an eigenvalue when the two eigenvalues are equal.
This is one more than the correct largest eigenvalue.
This is one less than the correct largest eigenvalue.
This is two more than the correct largest eigenvalue.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[b]] & [[a]] \end{pmatrix} \), a real symmetric matrix. What is its smallest eigenvalue?
solution: For \( A = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ b & a \end{pmatrix} \) the eigenvectors are \( (1,1) \) and \( (1,-1) \), giving eigenvalues \( a+b \) and \( a-b \). Here these are \( [[a]]+[[b]] \) and \( [[a]]-[[b]] \); since \( [[b]]>0 \), the smaller is \( [[a]]-[[b]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the eigenvalue \( a+b \), which is the LARGER one here since \( b>0 \).
This is the shared diagonal entry, the average of the two eigenvalues, not the smallest eigenvalue.
This doubles the diagonal entry rather than subtracting the correct offset \( b \).
This is the negative of the off-diagonal entry, not an eigenvalue of the matrix.
This is one less than the correct smallest eigenvalue.
This is two more than the correct smallest eigenvalue.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & 0 \\ [[b]] & [[a]] & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & [[e]] \end{pmatrix} \), a symmetric block-diagonal matrix. What is its smallest eigenvalue?
solution: The matrix is block-diagonal: the top-left \( 2\times2 \) symmetric block has eigenvalues \( [[a]]+[[b]] \) and \( [[a]]-[[b]] \), and the bottom-right \( 1\times1 \) block contributes the eigenvalue \( [[e]] \). The full eigenvalue set of \( A \) is the union of these three; the smallest is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is one eigenvalue of the top block, but not necessarily the overall smallest once the scalar block's eigenvalue \( e \) is included.
This is the other eigenvalue of the top block, but not necessarily the overall smallest.
This is the scalar block's eigenvalue, but not necessarily the overall smallest.
This is just the shared diagonal entry of the top block, not one of its actual eigenvalues in general.
This is one more than the correct smallest eigenvalue.
This is one less than the correct smallest eigenvalue.
